coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence resonance energy transfer biosensor based on carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for the detection of trypsin
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shaomei
Zhang, Fangmei
Xu, Longbin
Liu, Xin
Ma, Pinyi
Sun, Ying
Wang, Xinghua
Song, Daqian
coredata.dc:description: Herein we propose a fluorescence turn-on strategy for the sensitive and selective detection of trypsin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and amino-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots). In this assay, C-dots were treated as fluorometric reporter, while AuNPs were treated as fluorescence quencher. A specially designed negatively charged short peptide chain with one functioned cysteine group as the substrate for trypsin. The sensor works as follows: the negatively charged part of the peptide can be hydrolyzed by trypsin, resulting in the releasing of shorter and positively charged peptides. These shorter peptides then induces AuNPs aggregation due to the strong electrostatic interaction between positively charged peptides and the negatively charged AuNPs, thus leading to the restoration of the FRET-quenched fluorescence emission of C-dots. Measuring the changes in the fluorescence of C-dots, the concentrations of trypsin can be determined. Under the optimized conditions, we achieved quantitative evaluation of trypsin in a range of 2.5–80 ng mL−1 with the detection limit of 0.84 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, this sensing system also exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for trypsin, and we successfully applied it for the detection of trypsin in human serum samples. Based on the above findings, we conclude that this new FRET based sensor might be significant in disease diagnosis in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)
Carbon dots (C-dots)
Trypsin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of trypsin in human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405057 and 21207047 ), Science and Technology Developing Foundation of Jinlin Province of China (Nos. 20180201050YY , 201701011106JC , 20160623025TC and 20160204010GX ), Industrial Innovation Funds of Jilin Province of China (No. 2018C034-1 ), and Open Funds of the state Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (No. SKLEAC201704 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle of FRET-based sensing for determination of trypsin.

(a) TEM images of AuNPs: (a) in the absence of peptide and trypsin, (b) in the presence of 6.2 μM peptide, (c) in the presence of 6.2 μM peptide and 5 ng mL−1 trypsin, (d) in the presence of 6.2 μM peptide and 80 ng mL−1 trypsin.

(a) Zeta potentials of C-dots, AuNPs and peptide treated AuNPs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of (1) C-dots, (4) original AuNPs, (5) trypsin treated peptide-AuNPs, and (2) the fluorescence excitation spectrum of C-dots at λem = 455 nm, (3) the fluorescence emission spectrum of C-dots at λex = 360 nm.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of (1) C-dots, (2) peptide + C-dots, (3) peptide + AuNPs + trypsin + C-dots, (4) AuNPs + C-dots, (5) AuNPs + trypsin + C-dots, (6) peptide + AuNPs + C-dots.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of mixture containing peptide-treated AuNPs, C-dots and different amounts of trypsin (incubation time, 40 min). (b) Plots of fluorescence enhancement efficiency versus trypsin concentration.
Fluorescence response of the developed biosensor towards trypsin (concentration, 200 ng/mL) and common coexisting species (concentration of each species, 500 ng mL).
Detection of trypsin in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence resonance energy transfer biosensor based on carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for the detection of trypsin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we propose a fluorescence turn-on strategy for the sensitive and selective detection of trypsin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and amino-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots). In this assay, C-dots were treated as fluorometric reporter, while AuNPs were treated as fluorescence quencher. A specially designed negatively charged short peptide chain with one functioned cysteine group as the substrate for trypsin. The sensor works as follows: the negatively charged part of the peptide can be hydrolyzed by trypsin, resulting in the releasing of shorter and positively charged peptides. These shorter peptides then induces AuNPs aggregation due to the strong electrostatic interaction between positively charged peptides and the negatively charged AuNPs, thus leading to the restoration of the FRET-quenched fluorescence emission of C-dots. Measuring the changes in the fluorescence of C-dots, the concentrations of trypsin can be determined. Under the optimized conditions, we achieved quantitative evaluation of trypsin in a range of 2.5–80 ng mL−1 with the detection limit of 0.84 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, this sensing system also exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for trypsin, and we successfully applied it for the detection of trypsin in human serum samples. Based on the above findings, we conclude that this new FRET based sensor might be significant in disease diagnosis in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Proteases are protein degrading hydrolytic enzymes that involve in various biological and physiological process including digestion, blood coagulation, apoptosis and a series of other cellular activities [1,2]. As one of the most significant digestive protease, trypsin, produced in the pancreas, plays crucial role in numerous significant physiological processes such as cell differentiation, cell growth, immunological defense, and apoptosis [3]. The abnormal expression of trypsin can cause some pancreatic diseases such as pancreatic cancer, meconium ileus and hereditary pancreatitis, and so on [4]. Since trypsin is overexpressed in acute pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis and pancreatic cancer [5–7], it can be regarded as a biomarker for such illnesses. Therefore, developing a sensitive and selective method for trypsin detection is important.

Over the past years, many efforts, such as quartz crystal microbalance sensor [8], colorimetry [9,10], electrochemistry [11], fluorescence [7,12–15] and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [16,17], have been made to develop biosensors for trypsin detection. Among the reported methods, fluorescence-based methods have attracted special attention due to their high sensitivity, simple instruments, easy operation, and real-time detection. Specially, the fluorescence turn-on models are of particular interest owing to their less false signals, high specificity, and improved sensitivity [18,19]. Until now, several fluorescence turn-on strategy-based assays for trypsin have been reported. For example, Chen et al. [20] reported a fluorescence turn-on method for the detection of trypsin. The detection strategy functions as follows: (i) Cu2+ triggered fluorescence quenching of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), (ii) the cleavage of bovine serum albumin (BSA) by trypsin to release amino acids/peptide fragments, and (iii) stronger binding ability of amino acids/peptide fragments with Cu2+ than that between AuNCs and Cu2+, accompanied by the conversion from fluorescence off-state to on-state. Wang group [13] reported another trypsin detection method that involves immobilization of a fluorophore labeled peptide, containing an arginine and a terminal cysteine, onto the surface of gold nanoparticles. Upon the addition of trypsin, the peptide at C-terminus of arginine is hydrolyzed to release fluorophore from gold nanoparticles, leading to the strong fluorescence signal recovery. Using cytochrome C (Cyt C) to direct the synthesis of Mn-doped ZnS QDs, Wu et al. [12] obtained protein-conjugated QDs that were capable of protease sensing. However, these methods suffer from several limitations such as fluorescence labeling, high cost material, relatively high toxicity of quantum dots, and the use of traditional fluorescent dyes with low photostability. Hence, it is still imperative and significant to develop simple, sensitive and low toxic turn-on fluorescent method for trypsin detection.

Compared with traditional QDs and organic dyes, C-dots, as potential substrate for biosensing, have attracted considerable attention in various fields due to their high photostability, low toxicity, simple synthesis, water solubility and inexpensive raw materials [21–23]. These extraordinary properties make C-dots ideal fluorescent probes in FRET based biosensors [24–26]. On the other hand, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) often function as fluorescence quenchers due to their higher quenching efficiency in a wide spectral range which also overlaps with the emission of C-dots [27]. FRET occurs when the emission spectrum of a donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of an acceptor. FRET based on AuNPs/C-dots pair has been widely used in various fields such as immunoassay [28,29], nucleic acid detection [24,30,31], small molecule [31] and ion detection [32].

To the best of our knowledge, C-dots-based trypsin biosensor constructed by utilizing FRET between AuNPs and C-dots has never been reported. In this study, we have developed a sensitive and selective turn-on fluorescent probe for trypsin detection based on the FRET between C-dots and AuNPs. Scheme 1 illustrates the detection strategy of the proposed biosensor. A short peptide chain was designed as a substrate for trypsin. The peptides were immobilized on the surface of AuNPs through the –SH group of cysteine by forming Au–S bonds without inducing the aggregation of AuNPs. When C-dots were added to the peptide-treated AuNPs colloid, the fluorescence of C-dots was quenched by AuNPs. Trypsin easily cleaved the peptide at the site of Arginine (Arg) [33] to release a mixture of peptides with amino groups, which subsequently induced the aggregation of AuNPs, and, made the FRET-quenched fluorescence emission of C-dots restore. Thus, by evaluating the restored fluorescence emission intensity, one can quantitatively determine the presence of trypsin. A series of experiments were carried out to establish and verify the validity and applicability of the proposed biosensor. This method exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity than reported ones.
(Scheme 1)

UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Inc., USA) and a F-2700 spectrofluorophotometer (HITACHI Co., Ltd., Japan), respectively. A quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length was used in all experiments. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments were performed on a Hitachi H8100 electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV without staining. XPS was investigated by using ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). FT-IR spectra were recorded using KBr pellets on a Nicolet Avatar360 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Zeta potentials were measured on a Zetasizer (Malvern Co., UK) at room temperature. All pH values were measured with a PHS-3C pH-Meter (INESA Scientific Inc., China).

Peptide (Arg-Cys-Phe-Arg-Gly-Gly-Asp-Asp, RCFRGGDD) of 95% purity, trypsin and lysozyme were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Inc. (Shanghai, China). Pepsin, thrombin and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were procured from Ding Guo Biotechnology Company, China. Horse immunoglobulin M (IgG) as purchased from Beijing Biosynthesis Biotechnology Company. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Company Ltd., China. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) was purchased from Acros. Sodium citrate, ethylenediamine and citric acid were purchased from Tianjing Guangfu Chemical Company, China. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water from a Millipore purification device (18.2 MΩ cm) was used for all the syntheses and analyses.

The fluorescent C-dots were prepared using a simple microwave synthetic route according to a previous report with some modifications [34]. Typically, in the first step, 1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, followed by addition of 0.8 mL of ethylenediamine under vigorous stirring. The solution was then heated in a domestic 720 W microwave oven for 2 min, during which the solution changed from being a colorless liquid to a brown clustered solid, indicating the formation of C-dots. The obtained solid was dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500) for three days. The resultant C-dots were stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

AuNPs were synthesized through the reduction of HAuCl4 by sodium citrate [35]. Briefly, 100 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4 solution was heated to boiling under vigorous stirring, followed by quickly adding 10 mL of 38.8 mM trisodium citrate solution. The flask was allowed to be heated for another 20 min before cooling down to room temperature. The as-prepared AuNPs were then stored at 4 °C in refrigerator. The concentration of AuNPs was calculated by Lambert Beer’s law [36].

Trypsin standards of various concentrations were prepared by dissolving trypsin in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.5). Solutions consisting of peptide (6.2 μM) and trypsin (0–200 ng mL−1) were incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Then the mixtures were heated to 90 °C for 10 min to terminate cleavage reaction. Subsequently, 50 μL of the above solution was added to 250 μL of AuNPs colloid (5 nM). After gently shaking for 4 min, 50 μL of 0.35 mg mL−1 fluorescent C-dots were introduced to the reaction mixtures, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 2 min. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra (excited at 360 nm) of the mixtures were recorded.

To verify the utility of this method for complex matrix, spiked human serum samples were tested. Real human serums were diluted with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.5). Then, certain amounts of trypsin were added into the above solution to prepare spiked samples (spiked level of 5, 40, and 80 ng mL−1). Following the same experimental conditions and measurement procedures as the determination of trypsin in aqueous solution, the emission fluorescence at 455 nm was measured.

The corresponding TEM images revealed that the synthesized C-dots were of nearly spherical shape and well dispersed with an average size of 4 nm (Fig. S1). Lattice fringes of a C-dot were clearly shown in a high resolution TEM image (insert of Fig. S1). The presence of functional groups on surface of the synthesized C-dots was studied using FTIR spectra (Fig. S2). The absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1, 1651 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of NH. The absorption peaks at 1705 cm−1 and 1079 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and C–O bonds, respectively. To further confirm the structure information on the C-dots, XPS spectra were collected and investigated. The XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. S3a) shows three obvious peaks ascribed to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The C1 scan spectrum (Fig. S3b) exhibits peaks at 284.65 eV, 286 eV, and 279.8 eV, indicating the presence of CC, CN, and CO functional groups [37]. The N1 scan spectrum (Fig. S3c) shows strong signals from both amide-N and doping N atoms, which confirmed the successful surface coating of the C-dots by plenty of amine groups.

Fig. 1 illustrates the morphologies of the prepared AuNPs in the absence and presence of peptide and trypsin, respectively. TEM images revealed the synthesized AuNPs were uniform and monodisperse with an average diameter of 13 nm (Fig. 1a), and remained monodisperse in the presence of peptide (Fig. 1b). However, adding a small amount of trypsin (5 ng mL−1) to the peptide treated-AuNPs caused aggregation of AuNPs (Fig. 1c), and adding the same volume of trypsin with a higher concentration (80 ng mL−1) led to the more aggregation of AuNPs (Fig. 1d), confirming the cleavage of the peptide induced by trypsin.

The zeta potential of C-dots is + 10.1 mV, indicating the formation of positively charged C-dots, while a zeta potential of -13.3 mV for AuNPs suggests the formation of negatively charged nanoparticles (Fig. 2 a). The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged C-dots and the negatively charged AuNPs could bring them closer to meet the donor-acceptor distance demand of the FRET between C-dots and AuNPs. Fig. S4 demonstrates the physically mixed state of the aggregated AuNPs and the C-dots.

The optical properties of C-dots and AuNPs were investigated using both absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The characteristic absorption bands for the C-dots appear at 241 nm and 350 nm (spectrum 1), which can be ascribed to the large amount of π-electrons in the sp2-hybridized structures and the n-π* transition of C-dots, respectively [32]. The absorption band of AuNPs (spectrum 4) overlaps with the emission band of C-dots (spectrum 3), indicating that the C-dots and AuNPs can function as a donor-acceptor pair for a FRET system (Fig. 2b). Using quinine sulfate as the standard reference (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution), a quantum yield of 24% for the C-dots was obtained, suggesting C-dots as a potential highly sensitive energy donor. Furthermore, the effect of pH on the fluorescence property of C-dots was investigated. Results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots is slightly affected by pH at the range of 6.5–9.5 (Fig. S5).

According to the FRET mechanism, the emission of the donor would be quenched by the absorption of the acceptor when the following demands are met: (1) the emission and absorption bands are sufficiently overlapped, and (2) the donor and acceptor is in close proximity to each other (2–10 nm) [30,38]. Scheme 1 illustrates the proposed FRET-based sensing mechanism of this new biosensor constituting of C-dots and AuNPs, an energy donor-acceptor pair. Total charge of the original peptide is negative at pH 7.5 due to the two Asp residues in the sequence. The peptide gets attached onto the AuNPs through the –SH group on cysteine (forming Au–S bonds), which does not induce the aggregation of AuNPs due to the negatively charged property of the peptide (Scheme 1, path a). Therefore, addition of peptide has almost no effect on the FRET between AuNPs and C-dots. However, in the presence of trypsin, it catalyzes the hydrolysis of peptide immobilized on the AuNPs [33], forming shorter and positively peptides containing amino groups such as Arg-Cys-Phe-Arg-NH2 (RCFR) and Cys-Phe-Arg-NH2 (CFR). These cleaved peptides still immobilized on the AuNPs induce the aggregation of the AuNPs by the strong electrostatic interaction between the positively charged peptides and the negatively charged AuNPs (Scheme 1, path b), which decreases the probability of the electrostatic interaction as well as the FRET between AuNPs and C-dots, and results in the recovery of fluorescence emitted by C-dots effectively.

A series fluorescent experiments were carried out to confirm the proposed sensing mechanism. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , C-dots exhibit a strong fluorescence peak at 455 nm (spectrum 1). Upon addition of the peptide, no obvious change either in the fluorescence intensity or at the peak location was observed (spectrum 2), indicating there is no molecular interaction between peptide and C-dots. In the mixture of C-dots with AuNPs, a dramatic quenching of fluorescence emitted by C-dots was observed (spectrum 4) due to the FRET between AuNPs and C-dots. The peptide-treated AuNPs can induce more fluorescence quenching (spectrum 6) than original AuNPs, which may be due to the increasing negative charge density on AuNPs surface. The zeta potential of peptide treated-AuNPs is lower than the AuNPs. These results indicates that peptide was immobilized on the surface of AuNPs (Fig. 2a) due to the negative charge of the peptide. Control experiments showed that trypsin would not lead to the aggregation of original AuNPs (spectrum 5). However, upon the addition of trypsin to peptide-treated AuNPs, the aggregation of AuNPs occurred and the fluorescence intensity was largely restored (spectrum 3), confirming the peptide cleaving by trypsin. Fig. 2b shows an obvious red-shift of the absorption band (from 520 nm to 615 nm) of AuNPs in the presence of trypsin, which also confirmed that the cleavage of peptide induced the aggregation of AuNPs. Based on the above results, it can be inferred that the proposed FRET-based biosensor can detect trypsin efficiently.

After confirming the sensing ability of the proposed FRET-based biosensor for trypsin, several detection parameters such as concentrations of AuNPs and peptide, pH values of the medium, and incubation time for cleavage of peptide induced by trypsin, were further studied and optimized. The fluorescence enhancement efficiency (F-F0)/F0 as a criterion was employed to evaluate the sensing performance, where F and F0 correspond to the intensities of fluorescence emitted by C-dots in the presence and absence of trypsin, respectively.

Fig. S6a illustrates the typical fluorescence spectra of C-dots as a function of concentration of AuNPs in the range of 0.4–3.6 nM. The intensity of fluorescence emitted by C-dots gradually decrease with increasing the concentration of AuNPs. To obtain obvious efficiency of fluorescence enhancement and avoid excessive quencher, an AuNPs concentration of 3.6 nM with quenching efficiency of 74% for the fluorescence emission of C-dots was selected and used in the following experiments.

Further, the effect of peptide concentration on the detection of trypsin was we studied. The fluorescence enhanced efficiency increased with increasing the peptide concentration until the concentration reached 6.2 μM, then changed in the opposite trend (Fig. S7a). Hence, 6.2 μM was chosen as the optimum concentration of peptide for trypsin assay.

The pH value of a medium has been known to affect the interaction of AuNPs with small molecules [31]. Therefore, the effect of pH on the fluorescence enhanced efficiency was studied in the range of 6.5–9.5 (Fig. S7b). The maximal fluorescence enhancement efficiency was obtained at the pH value of 7.5. So, the following experiments were carried out at this optimized pH.

The incubation time for the hydrolysis of peptide by trypsin was examined (Fig. S7c). Results shows that the fluorescence enhanced efficiency increased quickly in the first 20 min, and reached a plateau after 40 min. Therefore, the ideal incubation time was set at 40 min.

Under the optimal conditions, the applicability of this FRET-based biosensor for the quantitative detection of trypsin was examined. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the intensity of fluorescence emitted by C-dots increased gradually with increasing trypsin concentration. Fig. 4b illustrates plots of the fluorescence enhancement factor against the concentration of trypsin. Good linearity was obtained in the trypsin concentration range of 2.5–80 ng mL−1. A limit of detection (LOD) of 0.84 ng mL−1 for trypsin, calculated based on three times of standard deviation of the blank signal (S/N = 3), was achieved, which is lower than that obtained in previous reports (Table S1).

Furthermore, we investigated the selectivity of this biosensor by screening the spectral responses towards some species coexisting with trypsin in the biological systems, such as bovine serum albumin (BSA), pepsin, lysozyme, thrombin, IgG, and ALP. As shown in Fig. 5 , this biosensor exhibited selective fluorescence recovery towards trypsin (200 ng mL−1) over other interferents (500 ng mL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of trypsin in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples spiked with different concentration of trypsin were examined by the developed biosensor to evaluate its practicality. Results listed in Table 1 shows that the recovery is in the range of 96.8–103.5% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 5%, indicating the credibility of this biosensor and its potential for trypsin quantification in clinical diagnosis.

In summary, we designed a fluorescence turn-on strategy based on FRET between peptide-treated AuNPs and C-dots. The peptides immobilized on the surface of AuNPs can be hydrolyzed by trypsin, which will induce the aggregation of AuNPs and suppress the FRET between AuNPs and C-dots subsequently. By monitoring the restored fluorescence emitted by C-dots, trypsin in biosamples can be determined. The biosensor developed based the above strategy exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for trypsin against other common coexisting species in biosamples with a LOD as low as 0.84 ng mL−1, demonstrating its potential for trypsin quantification in clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405057 and 21207047), Science and Technology Developing Foundation of Jinlin Province of China (Nos. 20180201050YY, 201701011106JC, 20160623025TC and 20160204010GX), Industrial Innovation Funds of Jilin Province of China (No. 2018C034-1), and Open Funds of the state Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (No. SKLEAC201704).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shaomei Xu is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Fangmei Zhang is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Longbin Xu is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. His interest is spectral analysis.
Xin Liu is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Pinyi Ma gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2017 and he is a senior engineer in that school. His research area is spectral analysis.
Ying Sun gained her doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2009 and she is an associate professor in that school. Her research area is spectral analysis.

Xinghua Wang gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2006 and he is an associate professor in that school. His research areas are spectral and chromatography analysis.
Daqian Song gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2003 and he is a professor in that school. His research areas are spectral and chromatography analysis.
